text: At the Obuasi deposit, the North orebody and No. 2 orebody are localized within fissure-controlled mineralized zones along a reverse fault, where hydrothermal alteration and deformation facilitated the introduction of plagioclase-bearing assemblages, thereby linking ore emplacement to structurally focused fluid pathways and fault-related permeability enhancement.

Mineralization was precipitated from a magmatic-hydrothermal fluid that evolved under a pressure of 2.0 kbar, with fluid inclusion data indicating an estimated pressure of 150 MPa, a homogenization temperature of 280 °C, a peak temperature of 420 °C, and subsequent cooling to temperature ranges from 98.2 to 135 °C, thereby recording progressive hydrothermal evolution during ore formation.

The Broken Hill deposit, situated along the southwestern margin of the Superior Craton, is interpreted within the metallogenic province as a structurally focused mineral system whose emplacement and preservation reflect the interplay of crustal architecture, metamorphic reworking, and long-lived tectono-magmatic processes characteristic of regionally endowed mining areas.

In the Gejiu deposit and the Talnakh deposit, albite commonly occurs in late-stage hydrothermal assemblages where Ca-rich alteration is overprinted by Rb- and Li-bearing fluids, whereas enargite is characteristic of high-sulfidation mineralization, reflecting strongly acidic, metal-rich conditions that promote the mobilization and precipitation of these elements within structurally controlled ore zones.

In the Zijinshan deposit, Olympic Dam deposit, and Jiama deposit, magnetite commonly occurs in close association with an argillic alteration zone, where hydrothermal fluids have overprinted host rocks and promoted iron oxide precipitation, thereby recording a geochemically evolved mineralizing system characteristic of structurally focused, porphyry-related to IOCG-affinity ore formation.

Within the platform margin adjacent to an oceanic trench, the Silurian metallogenic belt encompasses a major mining area and its southern mining area, where structurally controlled hydrothermal mineralization is exemplified by the Chuquicamata deposit and the Kelian deposit, both of which record distinct tectono-magmatic evolution and contribute to the regional ore-forming framework.

At the Ok Tedi deposit, mineralization is spatially controlled by a blind thrust fault and associated syncline, where the footwall orebody hosts phlogopite-bearing assemblages and the Veta Madre grades laterally into a stratiform orebody, indicating structurally focused hydrothermal emplacement and subsequent lithological stratification.

The Re-Os system provides robust geochronological constraints on mineralization at the Morenci deposit and the Sukari deposit, where biotite and microcline record hydrothermal alteration associated with ore formation, while cuprite commonly reflects supergene oxidation of primary copper sulfides, thereby linking isotopic signatures to the paragenetic evolution of these mineral deposits.

In the peripheral mining area of the Abitibi gold belt, a coherent GeochemicalAnomaly characterized by Y anomaly, Mn anomaly, and Hg anomaly, together with elevated Element concentrations of F and C, delineates a hydrothermal mineralizing system in the broader mining district, indicating strong fluid-rock interaction and potential vectors toward concealed ore-bearing structures.

Wall-rock alteration of mudstone and syenogranite records interaction with hydrothermal fluid and ore-forming fluid, during which muscovite crystallized as a diagnostic alteration mineral reflecting fluid-assisted metasomatism and the redistribution of components within the mineralizing system.

In the Bakken Formation, mineralization is localized where a detachment-related decollement, coupled with a listric fault geometry, focused fluid flow and structural dilation, thereby generating both an ore column along the fault conduit and a lens-shaped orebody in adjacent permeable strata.

The Pulang deposit is characterized by stibnite-bearing hydrothermal mineralization associated with silver enrichment, whereas the Porgera deposit hosts rutile-bearing assemblages and tin-bearing phases within structurally controlled ore systems, reflecting distinct metallogenic processes and fluid evolution in each deposit.

Mineral deposition is interpreted to have resulted from immiscibility and fluid mixing between a CO2-rich fluid and a CH4-bearing fluid at Th values of 220–260 °C and 250–320 °C, respectively, whereby interaction between a high-salinity brine with a salinity of 10 wt.% NaCl equiv. and a low-salinity fluid promoted destabilization of metal complexes and subsequent ore precipitation.

The Boddington deposit records an early ore stage within the broader ore-forming epoch, with geochronological constraints indicating a mineralization age of 235 ± 3 Ma for the principal event and a subsequent overprint at 221 ± 5 Ma, thereby defining a multi-pulsed metallogenic history consistent with protracted hydrothermal activity.

Mineral deposits on the Kola Peninsula are interpreted to reflect a complex metallogenic history in which lamprophyre and tuff intrusions and volcaniclastic horizons interact with the Monterey Formation and Mount Isa Group stratigraphic frameworks, thereby exerting strong structural and lithological controls on ore localization, fluid pathways, and post-depositional mineral enrichment.

The Comstock Lode comprises a breccia orebody that extends to 650 m below surface, with a true thickness of 6 m and an apparent thickness of 12 m, while its down-dip extent of 400 m encompasses an orebody depth range of 200 to 500 m and a broader thickness interval of 10–25 m, reflecting significant structural control on mineralization.

In the Carlin gold belt, mineral deposits are commonly localized where a transfer fault and associated fracture zone concentrate fluid flow and deformation, while kink band development further enhances permeability; analogous structural controls in the Nazca Plate margin can similarly focus hydrothermal circulation and promote ore-forming processes through repeated brittle-ductile reactivation.

The Toquepala deposit is genetically associated with granitic magmatism, whereby the granite host or intrusive source may localize hydrothermal alteration and ore-fluid circulation, while related mineralization styles include pegmatite deposit formation in late-stage felsic differentiates and, depending on skarn development at reactive contacts, iron skarn deposit and greisen tin-tungsten deposit assemblages.

The ore bed, developed within an amphibolite sequence in the foreland basin, is interpreted as a stratabound mineral deposit whose formation was controlled by basin-scale tectonic subsidence, lithological permeability contrasts, and subsequent hydrothermal fluid infiltration.

In a gold skarn deposit associated with an epithermal gold-silver deposit, a stockwork orebody may host an ore shoot and adjacent lenticular orebody where fluorite and dolomite occur as gangue and alteration minerals, reflecting structurally controlled fluid focusing, replacement processes, and localized metal precipitation within permeable lithologic and fracture networks.

The Kalgoorlie gold field and Bushveld mining area exemplify mineralized districts situated along craton margins and fold belts, respectively, where structurally controlled hydrothermal systems have generated world-class ore bodies such as the Grasberg deposit, Cortez deposit, and Kansanshi deposit through prolonged magmatic-hydrothermal activity, crustal deformation, and metal remobilization.

The ore district of the Val d'Or mining camp, situated within the East European Craton, hosts structurally controlled mineralization analogous to major deposits such as the Lihir deposit and the Sishen deposit, where contrasting magmatic-hydrothermal and banded iron formation-related ore-forming processes reflect the tectonic and metallogenic evolution of their respective crustal settings.

In the granitic gneiss and adjacent anorthosite, mineralization is spatially associated with potassic alteration and sericitization, with an inner carbonate alteration zone grading outward into a carbonate zone and locally overprinted by an argillic zone, collectively indicating progressive hydrothermal fluid–rock interaction and structural focusing of ore-forming fluids.

In the hypogene orebody, spodumene commonly crystallizes as a lithium-bearing pegmatitic mineral, whereas Ir and Be may be concentrated by hydrothermal differentiation and magmatic fractionation, respectively, reflecting complex geochemical partitioning within the same mineralized system.

The Red Dog deposit and Boddington deposit exemplify contrasting ore systems, with the former characterized by accessory tantalite, pyrochlore, and columbite hosting Nb and Tm in rare-metal assemblages, whereas the latter contains a copper-gold mineralization averaging 0.86% Cu and 850 ppb Au, reflecting distinct magmatic-hydrothermal and hydrothermal metal concentration processes.

The Carboniferous shale of the Hazelton Group is locally intruded by aplite and thermally metamorphosed into pyroxene hornfels, whereas during the Quaternary, supergene reworking of a phosphatic bed enhanced mineral concentration and redistribution within the adjacent sedimentary sequence.

Willemite and olivine occur within the host rock, where the diorite has undergone silicification and advanced argillic alteration, indicating a hydrothermal mineralizing system in which magmatic intrusion-driven fluid-rock interaction promoted metasomatism, recrystallization, and the localized concentration of these mineral phases.

The Talnakh deposit and the Muruntau deposit are economically significant mineral systems in which goethite commonly occurs as a secondary iron oxyhydroxide, while Cr and platinum may be concentrated in associated sulfide and noble-metal assemblages; notably, the Talnakh deposit also contains lead-bearing ore zones reported at grades of approximately 1.1% Pb, reflecting complex magmatic-hydrothermal and supergene overprints.

The Yanacocha deposit is a composite mineral system that includes Carlin-type gold, iron oxide-apatite, and high-sulfidation epithermal mineralization, with structural and hydrothermal overprinting of greenschist host rocks indicating complex fluid-rock interaction, metamorphic basement involvement, and multiple ore-forming stages within a long-lived magmatic-hydrothermal framework.

In the study area, the polymetallic vein occupies a synform and is spatially associated with a NW-trending extensional fracture system, whereas the ore seam is localized along a normal fault with NNE strike, indicating that deformation-controlled fluid pathways and structural anisotropy governed ore emplacement within the nearly EW-trending mineralized corridor.

In the Kiruna mining district within the broader iron belt, a pronounced geochemical anomaly in H and yttrium indicates hydrothermal reworking of the host stratigraphy, suggesting that element enrichment was controlled by structurally focused fluid flow and may delineate prospective mineralized zones associated with iron oxide–apatite deposit formation.

At the Lihir deposit and the Voisey's Bay deposit, mineralization is spatially associated with tonalite intrusions, sedimentary breccia, and greisen alteration, and is further localized by a regional fold that enhanced fluid focusing, structural permeability, and the emplacement of ore-bearing hydrothermal systems.

The breccia-pipe orebody comprises a proximal stringer zone that grades downward into an ore column, with the mineralized interval attaining a thickness of 0.8 to 3.2 m and locally narrowing to 0.6 m, while the system extends to 650 m below surface, indicating significant vertical continuity and structural control.

The mineralizing basinal brine, characterized by salinity ranges from 4.15 to 13.26 wt.% NaCl equivalent, records early fluid inclusion homogenization at Th of 135 °C, followed by higher Th values of 220–260 °C, and culminates in a trapping temperature of 300 °C, indicating progressive heating during ore formation.

text: At the Jwaneng deposit, carbon isotopes preserved in K-feldspar record fluid–rock interaction in a kimberlitic system, whereas at the Las Bambas deposit, neon isotopes in chrysocolla constrain supergene fluid sources, and at the Tarkwa deposit, braunite associated with gold-bearing conglomerates reflects oxidized sedimentary diagenesis within the paleoplacer succession.

The Main orebody, footwall orebody, and No. 3 orebody host structurally controlled mineralization in which U and palladium are locally concentrated within assemblages dominated by franklinite, argentite, and arsenopyrite, indicating a multistage hydrothermal system in which sulfide deposition and element remobilization were focused along orebody margins and adjacent wall-rock contacts.

In the Jiangbian deposit and the Jiama deposit, the Pb-Zn mineralization stage is interpreted as a Yanshanian mineralization ore-forming event dated at ca. 73 Ma, broadly comparable to the metallogenic timing recognized for the Kidd Creek deposit, thereby indicating a temporally focused pulse of mineralization within a convergent tectonomagmatic framework.

During water-fluxed melting and subsequent mantle metasomatism, mineralizing systems may evolve to exsolve a boiling hydrothermal fluid that precipitates ore assemblages upon cooling from a peak temperature of 510 C to 250-320 °C, thereby recording the progressive physicochemical transition from magmatic to epithermal conditions.

In the pyroxenite and gabbro host rock units, mineralization is spatially associated with a CO2-rich fluid that promoted silicification, tourmalinization, and albitization, thereby generating structurally controlled alteration halos and enhancing the deposition of sphalerite and chalcocite along fractures and permeable zones.

In the Dachang deposit and Antamina deposit, arsenopyrite occurs as a sulfide-bearing ore phase intimately associated with epidote-rich alteration assemblages, electrum is locally developed in precious-metal mineralization, and trace iridium enrichment reflects high-temperature magmatic-hydrothermal processes that overprinted the evolving polymetallic system.

Mineralization is localized where a nonconformity intersects the dome structure, with ore-controlling faults displaying a northwest strike that is compatible with an ENE-WSW strike set, whereas bedding and vein attitudes record a strike of N45E and dips of 40°, 65°, and 70 degrees, collectively indicating steeply inclined structural conduits favorable for fluid ascent and deposit formation.

The Cu-Mo geochemical anomaly delineated within the uranium belt suggests a hydrothermally modified metallogenic system, in which enrichment of Rh may reflect magmatic-sulfidic fractionation or remobilization processes associated with structurally focused fluid pathways and the potential development of polymetallic mineral deposits.

At the Cannington deposit, δ66Zn values recorded in sphalerite and associated pyrargyrite, together with halite-bearing evaporitic assemblages, indicate isotope fractionation linked to hydrothermal fluid evolution and metal sulfide precipitation, thereby constraining the physicochemical conditions governing Zn redistribution and ore formation within this polymetallic mineral system.

The Kiruna deposit, a major iron oxide–apatite orebody, is situated within a stable craton and can be geographically referenced by the provided coordinates, specifically UTM Zone 47N Northing 3298760 and the reported latitude-longitude positions of 26.42 N, 100.91 E and 31.25°N, 103.47°E, which collectively constrain its regional geologic setting and spatial distribution.

At the Neves-Corvo deposit, the nepheline syenite hosts mineralization spatially associated with an alteration assemblage comprising a chlorite zone and an albitic zone, where hydrothermal overprinting locally concentrated native bismuth and wulfenite along structurally controlled fractures and replacement fronts, indicating a complex magmatic-hydrothermal evolution.

In the dunite, interaction with a CO2-rich fluid produced silica-carbonate alteration and K-feldspathization, whereas adjacent pegmatite localized late-stage mineralization by acting as a structurally and compositionally distinct host that focused fluid flow and enhanced chemical gradients conducive to deposit formation.

The Antamina deposit, a major porphyry copper-skarn system of the Andean copper belt metallogenic province, formed during the Cenozoic along an active margin, and it exemplifies the spatial and temporal controls on mineralization that are also observed in other convergent-margin settings such as Kyrgyzstan.

At the Lihir deposit, a collapse breccia pipe developed within a transtensional shear zone that was later reactivated by a dextral strike-slip fault, localizing an ore vein and adjacent lenticular orebody in structurally dilatant sites where sylvanite and pitchblende precipitated from hydrothermal fluids.

The Dapingzhang deposit, situated at UTM Northing 3789123, is interpreted as a structurally controlled mineral system developed in a continental rift setting that was later overprinted by orogenic deformation, thereby linking extensional basin evolution with subsequent compressional reactivation in a geodynamically complex metallogenic framework.

Core samples from the Pueblo Viejo deposit and the Obuasi deposit, together with mineralized rock sample and powdered sample analyses, reveal skutterudite as a hydrothermal arsenide phase associated with sulfide-rich alteration, indicating localized metal enrichment within structurally controlled ore zones and providing key mineralogical constraints on deposit genesis and paragenesis.

Mineral deposits hosted in the Cambrian Belt Supergroup and associated arkose commonly record sedimentary reworking and basin evolution, whereas kimberlite intrusions emplaced during the Late Cretaceous may transport mantle-derived minerals to the surface, and evaporite formation sequences of Ordovician age can concentrate economically significant saline and metallic brines through diagenetic remobilization.

In the Platreef, a massive sulfide orebody hosts a disseminated zone in which pitchblende and galena occur as discrete sulfide-associated minerals, whereas chrysotile is developed in altered ultramafic wall rocks; geochemical enrichment of phosphorus and bismuth reflects hydrothermal remobilization and metalliferous overprinting within the orebody.

During the ore-forming epoch, stage III mineralization was overprinted by a post-ore stage characterized by fault reactivation and hydrothermal precipitation, which promoted the localized deposition and alteration of tremolite along structurally controlled conduits, thereby recording the evolving physicochemical conditions of the mineral system.

Mineralogical and isotopic constraints indicate that the deposit evolved from a high-salinity brine to an H2O-CO2 fluid, with inclusion fluid data recording a trapping temperature of 300 °C and very low salinity of 1.5 wt.% NaCl equiv., while δ30Si values, the Ar-Ar system, and thorium isotopes collectively suggest that mineralization postdated the peak temperature of 420 °C and was subsequently overprinted during a 250-320 °C hydrothermal stage.

spans.text: very low salinity of 1.5 wt.% NaCl equiv.

text: Wolframite mineralization is commonly hosted by basalt and dolerite units where hydrothermal alteration manifests as epidotization adjacent to vein zones, sericitization within feldspathic groundmass, and argillization in more intensely leached domains, collectively indicating a structurally controlled alteration halo favorable for tungsten deposition.

During the Late Cretaceous-Paleocene Laramide orogeny and subsequent geodynamic evolution, weathering of pyroxenite, rhyolitic tuff, and micrite promoted hydrothermal alteration characterized by kaolinization, silicification, and K-feldspathization, thereby enhancing the concentration and redistribution of mineral deposits within the evolving crustal system.

Hematitization in the quartz diorite, siltstone, and mudstone is interpreted to record multistage interaction with a hydrothermal fluid, whereby an early low-salinity aqueous fluid facilitated wall-rock alteration and permeability enhancement, followed by invasion of a high-salinity ore fluid that promoted iron mobilization, precipitation, and localized mineral deposition.

In the Tianshan metallogenic belt mining area, mineralization comprises porphyry deposit systems, low-sulfidation epithermal deposit assemblages, and porphyry tin deposit occurrences, reflecting a polyphase magmatic-hydrothermal evolution; analogous deposit associations are documented in Yunnan, Northern Territory, and Jilin, where regional tectono-magmatic frameworks exert a first-order control on ore-fluid focusing and metal enrichment.

The mineralizing fluid was interpreted as a CO2-bearing aqueous fluid and an N2-bearing hydrothermal fluid, with a homogenization temperature of 165 C and a peak temperature of 510 C, indicating progressive fluid evolution under elevated pressure conditions of several kbar and the concentration of ore metals reaching 980 ppb, ultimately contributing to mineralization at grades of g/t scale.

Within Western Australia on the Indo-Australian Plate, a copper-bearing bed occurs conformably within an evaporite sequence and is overlain by a terrestrial sequence, while adjacent graywacke is structurally overprinted by mylonite and crosscut by kimberlite, collectively recording sedimentary, tectonic, and magmatic processes that controlled mineral deposit localization.

In the andesite host rock, calaverite occurs as an economically significant Au–Te phase localized within zones of tourmaline-quartz alteration, whereas amphibole is variably overprinted by albitization, indicating a hydrothermal system in which early magmatic silicic alteration was subsequently modified by sodic metasomatism.

At the Ok Tedi deposit, barite commonly occurs with cobalt- and manganese-bearing sulfide assemblages, whereas the Talnakh deposit hosts proustite and tremolite within complex magmatic-hydrothermal ore zones, reflecting distinct paragenetic associations and contrasting physicochemical conditions during mineralization.

The No. 2 orebody is a moderately dipping mineral deposit with a dip angle of 40°, locally attaining a steep dip of 80 degrees, and it exhibits a strike length of 95 m with continuity over 600 m, while its thickness varies from an average width of 6.5 m to a maximum width of 30 m, indicating substantial lateral and vertical geometric persistence.

At the Qulong deposit in western China, an Ag anomaly, a rock geochemical anomaly, and a Pb-Zn anomaly collectively delineate a coherent mineralized halo analogous to geochemical signatures observed around the Simandou deposit and the McArthur River deposit, thereby indicating metallogenic processes that concentrate ore elements through hydrothermal alteration and structural control.

The Century deposit comprises a polymetallic sulfide assemblage in which corundum may occur as an accessory mineral within alumina-rich alteration zones, while trace concentrations of ruthenium, boron, and copper reflect magmatic-hydrothermal fractionation, fluid-rock interaction, and late-stage metasomatic overprinting during ore genesis.

At the Venetia deposit, Toquepala deposit, and Kansanshi deposit, magnetite is spatially and genetically associated with alteration assemblages that include an illite zone, a potassic alteration zone, and a tourmaline alteration zone, respectively, reflecting hydrothermal overprinting and fluid-rock interaction during ore formation.

In mineral deposit studies, in-situ Pb isotope analysis, integrated with the C-O isotope system, can discriminate fluid sources and metal provenance, while geochronological constraints from a molybdenite Re-Os age of 160.3 ± 1.7 Ma and the broader 73 to 56 Ma interval collectively refine the temporal evolution of ore-forming hydrothermal events.

In a doubly polished section of the vein quartz sample, mineralogical textures indicate that chloritization affects the adjacent granitic gneiss and black shale, while potassic alteration and pervasive silicification are developed within the Triassic granitoid plutons, collectively recording a multistage hydrothermal overprint associated with mineral deposit formation.

During the MetallogenicTime main mineralization stage, native silver was concentrated in association with metamorphism and partial melting, processes that reworked pre-existing sulfide-bearing assemblages, while low-angle subduction provided the tectonic setting for enhanced fluid-rock interaction, crustal thickening, and efficient metal remobilization within the evolving ore-forming system.

At the Voisey's Bay deposit and the McArthur River deposit, the AlterationZone: kaolinite zone is spatially associated with hydrothermal overprinting, while Mineral: siderite, limonite, and zircon occur as diagnostically distinct phases reflecting supergene oxidation, gangue precipitation, and resistant detrital input within the evolving ore system.

During the quartz-carbonate stage, Laramide magmatism, contact metamorphism, and subsequent tectonism collectively promoted the remobilization and localized precipitation of monazite and cuprite, indicating that the deposit evolved through a multistage hydrothermal overprint in which late-stage fluid-rock interaction and structural reactivation controlled the final mineral assemblages.

The Morenci deposit, Yulong deposit, and Goldstrike deposit exhibit contrasting metal endowments, with reported grades including 1800 ppm Zn and 12.6% TFe, and mineralized intervals commonly constrained by ranges between 1.1 and 2.7 wt.%, not less than 15 m in thickness, and 1.2–2.5, reflecting significant geological heterogeneity in ore distribution and tenor.

Mineralization was precipitated from a magmatic-hydrothermal fluid at an ore fluid temperature of 290 °C within a broader thermal regime of 250–320 °C and 281 to 371 °C, while fluid inclusion data indicate moderate salinity of 8–12 wt.% NaCl equiv., suggesting a metal-transporting hydrothermal system evolved under conditions conducive to efficient ore deposition.

The Fosterville deposit is interpreted to have formed during geodynamic evolution associated with fluid-fluxed melting along a passive continental margin of Laurasia within the broader tectonic framework of Pangea, where regional compression and magmatic-hydrothermal fluid migration promoted metal enrichment and ore precipitation.

Mineral deposits may form where underplating and magma mixing drive hydrothermal circulation beneath compressional deformation, promoting tourmalinization in pillow basalt, decarbonatization of carbonate-bearing host rocks, and silica-carbonate alteration along structures where phyllite provides a competent, foliated conduit for fluid focusing and metal precipitation.

Dy is commonly concentrated in hydrothermal mineral deposits where chromite and manganite occur within a tabular orebody, and localized enrichment of Dy may be further controlled by structurally focused ore shoot development, reflecting strong lithological and structural partitioning during ore-forming processes.

In the Jiangbian deposit, drill-core samples and associated geologic specimens of altered rock commonly preserve graphite-rich assemblages in which diopside and tourmaline occur as metamorphic-hydrothermal minerals, indicating fluid-rock interaction during alteration and providing key constraints on the deposit’s paragenesis and mineralizing evolution.

The Cadia deposit records an ore-forming stage in which the main gold-mineralizing stage is temporally constrained by zircon U-Pb ages of 246 ± 4 Ma and a magmatic age of 231 ± 4 Ma, while syn-ore alteration and mineralization are further indicated by a syn-ore age of 79–72 Ma, collectively defining a complex multistage metallogenic evolution.

The GeophysicalAnomaly comprises a resistivity anomaly coincident with a linear EM anomaly that delineates the OreBody, including an ore lens and the East orebody, which together exhibit a drill intercept at 1350 m depth and indicate a mineralized system extending to a depth of 450 m with a down-dip extent of 400 m.

In the mineral deposit, the ore-forming fluid evolved from an H2O-CO2-NaCl fluid to an H2O-CO2 fluid, and fluid inclusion data indicate a trapping temperature of 300 °C, whereas isotopic constraints from the 147Sm/144Nd system record a peak temperature of 510 C, collectively suggesting progressive fluid-rock interaction during ore formation.

Mineralization associated with cobaltite and beryl occurs within hydrothermal vein assemblages, where the ore grade reaches 6.8 g/t Ag and spatial variability is constrained by a compositional range of 1.2–2.5, indicating localized metal enrichment under structurally controlled geological conditions.

In the studied mineral deposit, the NNW-trending transfer fault truncates a phyllonite zone that localizes the Main Reef Leader, while a breccia-pipe orebody developed adjacent to the fault intersection and a supergene orebody occupies the weathered upper levels where the host structure strikes to the northeast, indicating strong structural control on ore emplacement and subsequent enrichment.

Microthermometric and inclusion data indicate that the mineralizing system evolved from an aqueous fluid to a boiling hydrothermal fluid and finally to a CaCl2-rich basinal brine, with a measured Th of 135 °C and entrapment under moderate lithostatic conditions of 1.5 kbar, consistent with a trapping pressure of 1.2–2.0 kbar and an estimated trapping pressure of 2.4 kbar.

The OreBody pay shoot is hosted within a vein with a vein length of 250 m, traceable for 800 m along strike, and exhibits a true width of 7.5 m, an average width of 11 m, and local widths ranging from 9–18 m, while the mineralized structure dips at 65°, indicating a steeply inclined ore lens with substantial lateral continuity.

The Late Triassic Cadia deposit in the Jiangxi–Queensland metallogenic corridor, the Late Jurassic Collahuasi deposit associated with the Zambian Copperbelt, and the Tara deposit within the Olympic Dam mining area collectively illustrate the spatiotemporal variability of mineralization processes, reflecting distinct tectonic settings and hydrothermal regimes across these globally distributed mineral provinces.

In a mineralized tuff sample from the Escondida deposit, integrated Pb isotope analysis, EPMA analysis, and Raman microspectrometry can be used to constrain the paragenesis and provenance of xenotime and bismuthinite, while analogous geochemical and mineralogical protocols applied to the Kamoa-Kakula deposit and Kidd Creek deposit enable robust comparison of ore-forming processes across distinct metallogenic systems.

During slab rollback, water-fluxed partial melting in the mantle wedge can generate a mineralizing fluid that ascends into extensional structures and, upon cooling to a trapping temperature of 300 °C, precipitates ore-forming minerals from hydrothermal solutions.

The Kelian deposit, Alumbrera deposit, and McArthur River deposit exemplify contrasting mineralizing systems, with the Kelian deposit representing an epithermal deposit, the Alumbrera deposit hosted in structurally modified mylonite, and the McArthur River deposit illustrating a supergene enrichment deposit, thereby highlighting the influence of deformation, hydrothermal fluid flow, and secondary remobilization on ore concentration.

The Kiruna deposit hosts mineralization characterized by 0.86% Cu and 0.23 wt.% Mo, with native gold and dolomite occurring as constituent phases within the ore assemblage, while trace concentrations of Lu and praseodymium indicate enrichment in incompatible elements and suggest complex hydrothermal overprinting of the deposit’s magmatic-hydrothermal system.

At the Ranger deposit, the North orebody comprises a massive sulfide lens localized along a tear fault and adjacent tension gashes that exploit an unconformity surface, whereas the disseminated orebody is spatially more distal and hosts cassiterite and willemite as accessory minerals within structurally prepared host rocks.

Mineralization is interpreted to have precipitated from an ore fluid temperature of 290 C, evolving from an early inclusion fluid and aqueous fluid to a CH4-bearing fluid under conditions of 250-320 °C and a brine salinity of 22.0 wt.% NaCl equiv., while the sulfur isotope signature (Δ33S) records interaction with a higher-temperature hydrothermal system at a peak temperature of 420 °C.

text: Mineralization in the Yixian Formation, particularly within calc-silicate rock horizons, is interpreted to have developed during the Late Cretaceous to Paleocene interval, when regional tectonothermal overprinting and fluid-rock interaction promoted metasomatic redistribution of ore-forming elements and the localized concentration of economically significant mineral deposits.

Mineral deposition is interpreted to have resulted from a CO2-bearing aqueous fluid as the principal mineralizing fluid, with fluid-inclusion data indicating trapping at a homogenization temperature of 280 °C and a broader equilibration interval of 250–320 °C, whereas late-stage overprinting occurred at temperatures ranging from 98.2 to 135 °C under a pressure range of 0.8–1.6 kbar and, locally, pressure below 1 kbar.

Supergene enrichment of the deposit was promoted by a high-salinity ore fluid, which precipitated magnetite and pyrite under a trapping temperature of 300 °C and a homogenization temperature of 280 °C, while andalusite records an earlier peak temperature of 420 °C, collectively constraining the thermal evolution and ore-forming history of the system.

The Cerro Verde deposit is a giant porphyry deposit hosted within altered intrusive assemblages adjacent to gneissic basement, whereas the Porgera deposit is genetically associated with skarn-style mineralization developed in contact-metamorphosed pyroxene hornfels, and together they contrast with BHT deposit models that typically reflect sediment-hosted hydrothermal precipitation rather than magmatic-hydrothermal ore formation.

The pyrite sample occurs within a sericite-quartz schist and is associated with hydrothermal alteration characterized by pervasive silicification and subordinate chloritization, indicating fluid-rock interaction that overprinted the host lithology and promoted sulfide deposition along metamorphic foliation and microfractures.

Laramide magmatism and associated dehydration melting generated an ore-forming hydrothermal system in which a N2-bearing hydrothermal fluid, characterized by an ore fluid temperature of 290 C and a homogenization temperature of 165 C, precipitated spodumene and sillimanite, indicating a high-temperature, metamorphically overprinted mineralizing environment.

Mineral deposition was driven by an ore-forming fluid and a CO2-rich fluid evolving into a magmatic-hydrothermal fluid, with fluid inclusions indicating a trapping temperature of 345 C and a peak temperature of 510 C, whereas mineralization in the host unit occurred at 250-320 °C and produced grades of up to g/t, with alteration locally centered near 285 C.

In the Olympic Dam deposit, the outer propylitic zone and the inner propylitic zone constitute alteration halos that spatially and genetically frame the ore system, within which bornite occurs as a प्रमुख copper sulfide phase and acanthite represents a late-stage silver-bearing mineral, together recording progressive hydrothermal alteration and metal enrichment.

The Jwaneng deposit, Malmberget deposit, and Jabiluka deposit illustrate contrasting ore-system architectures, with reported grades of 3.8 g/t Au, 1.1% Pb, and 0.23 wt.% Mo, respectively, and sulfur-isotope compositions spanning δ³⁴S ≈ 0 ± 3.0 ‰ and 0.3–0.9%, values that collectively constrain source reservoirs, hydrothermal fluid evolution, and mineralizing redox conditions.

Ore deposition within granodiorite intrusions may be genetically linked to mantle metasomatism and subsequent fluid-fluxed melting, whereby metal-bearing magmas or hydrothermal fluids interact with quartz arenite country rocks, producing pervasive sericitization and later carbonatization as diagnostic alteration halos around economically significant mineral deposits.

In eclogite and phonolite host rocks, potassic alteration is commonly overprinted by late-stage basinal brine and CH4-bearing fluid infiltration, with aqueous fluid-mediated remobilization of metals promoting the deposition of argentite and the supergene development of jarosite along fractures and alteration halos.

The mineral deposits are interpreted to have formed from a magmatic fluid characterized by elevated 3He/4He and distinctive lead isotopes, with fluid-inclusion homogenization temperatures of 165 C indicating late-stage cooling, whereas mineralization elsewhere records higher thermal conditions of 281 to 371 °C and a peak temperature of 420 °C, collectively reflecting a progressive magmatic-hydrothermal evolution.

Carbon isotopic data, together with fluid inclusion microthermometry, indicate that the mineral deposit formed through interaction of a CaCl2-rich basinal brine and a magmatic-hydrothermal fluid with a CO2-rich fluid, recording a homogenization temperature of 280 °C and a trapping temperature of 345 C, while salinities range from very low salinity of 1.5 wt.% NaCl equiv. to 6.2–9.7 wt.% NaCl equiv., consistent with multiphase fluid mixing and ore precipitation.

text: In the Cadia deposit, Pulang deposit, and Grasberg deposit, apatite and hematite are commonly associated with syenogranite-hosted alteration assemblages, where a silicified zone grades outward into a hydrothermal alteration halo and then a wall-rock alteration halo, reflecting progressive fluid-rock interaction and metallogenic zonation.

At the Lihir deposit, a pronounced high chargeability anomaly identified by induced polarization delineates an IP anomaly spatially associated with a listric fault, suggesting that structurally focused fluid flow and sulfide accumulation along the fault geometry were critical controls on ore localization.

In the Muruntau deposit, comparative petrographic analysis of a fresh granite sample, powdered sample, and host-rock sample can elucidate alteration and mineralization pathways, wherein epidote commonly records hydrothermal overprint in the host-rock sample, whereas diamond, if present as an exceptional phase, would indicate profound high-pressure provenance or contamination not typically associated with granitic assemblages.

In the Alpine-Himalayan orogenic belt, mineralization at an active margin is commonly expressed by a remote-sensing alteration anomaly and associated spectral anomaly that delineate a concentric alteration framework, with a central phyllic zone grading outward into an intermediate argillic zone and a peripheral ferric iron alteration zone, thereby reflecting progressive hydrothermal fluid-rock interaction.

The Jiama ore district and the Broken Hill mining district exemplify structurally controlled mineral provinces, whereas the Boddington deposit represents a large disseminated ore system hosted within an orogenic terrane, and comparable metallogenic signatures are recognized in shield settings such as British Columbia, where regional tectonism, magmatism, and hydrothermal alteration collectively govern ore localization.

In the Century deposit and Resolution deposit, a Riedel shear developed above a regional decollement and adjacent to a monocline structurally localized both a massive orebody and a hydrothermal breccia-pipe orebody, with oxidation and supergene overprint precipitating braunite and goethite along permeable fracture networks and breccia matrices.

The Au-Ag geochemical anomaly in the Guiana Shield and Australia is spatially associated with the Rössing deposit and the Yanacocha deposit, respectively, where hydrothermal alteration, metal enrichment, and structurally controlled fluid flow collectively indicate a strong genetic link between the observed geochemical signatures and ore-forming magmatic-hydrothermal systems.

In the diorite and anorthosite host rocks, mineralization is commonly associated with greisenization, K-feldspathization, and albitization produced by an evolving hydrothermal fluid and a CO2-rich fluid, whose metasomatic interaction promoted feldspar replacement, silica enrichment, and the concentration of economically significant mineral deposits.

In the Jinchuan deposit, hornfels locally overprint the host sequence adjacent to an ultramylonite zone, while later extensional fractures preferentially channel hydrothermal fluids and redistribute sulfides, thereby recording a multistage tectono-metamorphic evolution and structural control on ore localization.

Within the Slave Craton hinterland, the phosphorite mineralization occurs as a mineralized bed within a sedimentary group and is spatially associated with a volcanic-sedimentary sequence, indicating that depositional and volcanogenic processes collectively controlled the stratigraphic localization and concentration of phosphate-bearing sediments.

In the Qinling metallogenic belt mining camp, economically significant mineralization is spatially and genetically associated with a fault contact that transects the large igneous province, where repeated tectono-magmatic reactivation focused hydrothermal fluid flow, promoted wall-rock alteration, and facilitated ore precipitation along structurally weakened zones.

In the Jiama ore district, a lake-sediment anomaly of GeochemicalAnomaly type is characterized by enrichment in Element O and carbon, indicating a geochemical dispersion halo that likely reflects oxidative-surface weathering and hydrothermal mineralization processes associated with concealed mineral deposits.

Mineral deposition was driven by an H2O-NaCl fluid that evolved into a boiling hydrothermal fluid, with homogenization indicating trapping temperatures of 300 °C within a broader range of 250–320 °C and 281 to 371 °C, while fluid inclusion constraints suggest entrapment at a fluid pressure of 1.8 kbar under conditions approaching a lithostatic pressure of 3 kbar.

During the Oligocene, mineralization was spatially associated with the carbonate group and adjacent intrusive and altered host rocks, including trachyte, tourmalinite, and nepheline syenite, whose contrasting lithologic and geochemical characteristics likely promoted fluid focusing, metasomatic alteration, and the concentration of ore-forming elements within structurally controlled zones.

The mineral deposit comprises pitchblende occurring as high-grade ore with an average zinc content of 1800 ppm Zn, and the mineralized zone extends over a thickness of not less than 15 m, indicating a laterally persistent and economically significant uranium-bearing assemblage.

In the agglomerate-hosted wall-rock sample and adjacent pelitic schist, pervasive wall-rock alteration is overprinted by tourmaline-quartz alteration, whereas the quartz sample records a late-stage hydrothermal alteration assemblage, collectively indicating progressive fluid-rock interaction and localized silica-tourmaline enrichment within the mineral deposit system.

During the Laramide orogeny, tectonism promoted hydrothermal circulation that focused metal-bearing fluids into structurally dilated zones, where early ore stage assemblages precipitated chalcopyrite alongside calaverite, followed by stage III mineralization characterized by late deposition of native sulfur from cooling, evolving hydrothermal fluids.

The Jabiluka deposit and the Cuajone deposit are both spatially associated with a brittle shear zone, where a negative geophysical anomaly and a coincident electrical anomaly collectively indicate structurally focused fluid pathways, enhanced permeability, and hydrothermal alteration processes conducive to ore localization.

Within the Wyoming Craton, the copper belt hosts mineralized systems whose tectono-stratigraphic evolution records a Cambrian basement inheritance subsequently overprinted by Oligocene hydrothermal activity, a temporal framework comparable to the multistage metallogenesis of the Antamina deposit, where episodic fluid flow and structural reactivation concentrated economically significant ore assemblages.

In the study of mineral deposits, a polished thin section and zircon separate were integrated with remote sensing and SIMS analysis to constrain alteration patterns and U–Pb geochronology, while an electron microprobe and Raman spectrometer were used to determine mineral chemistry and structural characteristics with high analytical precision.

Mineralization is localized within a fracture zone that exploits a joint set, where the ENE-WSW strike and strike of 045 degrees define the structural fabric, and the zone dips 60° to 75° with a dip of 70 degrees, indicating a steeply dipping geometry that likely enhanced fluid focusing and ore precipitation.

The anorthosite host rock is commonly dissected by fracture networks and deformed into parasitic folds, structural features that can localize hydrothermal fluids and enhance ore emplacement in the Zijinshan deposit, the Sukari deposit, and the Platreef deposit, where such lithological and structural controls are critical for concentrating mineralization.

At the El Teniente deposit, the West orebody and associated stratiform orebody are spatially organized around a dome structure that localizes an intrusive contact and overprints an unconformity, whereas the Dexing deposit preserves a comparable stringer zone beneath its principal orebody, with goethite indicating supergene alteration along structurally controlled mineralized pathways.

Mineralization is interpreted to have evolved from a low-salinity aqueous fluid at a pressure of 210 MPa and a peak temperature of 510 C, with subsequent overprinting by a high-salinity ore fluid under comparable hydrothermal conditions, whereas equilibrium calculations indicate trapping near a pressure of 2.0 kbar, consistent with progressive fluid evolution during deposit formation.

Mercury isotopes in the mineral deposits indicate that ore precipitation was governed by a metamorphic fluid overprinted by a H2O-CO2-NaCl fluid, with fluid inclusion microthermometry yielding a homogenization temperature of 280 °C and a lower Th of 135 °C, collectively recording evolving hydrothermal conditions during mineralization.

The mineralized assemblage is dominated by enargite and tetrahedrite, with assay data indicating a high-grade system characterized by 5.4 g/t Au, 1.4% Cu equivalent, and 12.6% TFe, while geochemical and textural variability is reflected in component concentrations reaching as much as 4.6%, grain-size or abundance ranges of 10 to 30, and isotopic compositions spanning −7.29 to +3.18 ‰.

Topaz commonly forms during the syn-ore stage of mineralization and may be stabilized in stage II mineralization associated with contact metamorphism, whereas subsequent post-ore stage supergene enrichment can modify the deposit geochemically by enhancing alteration products and redistributing trace constituents around the original topaz-bearing assemblage.

The Timmins mining camp, situated within a volcanic arc on the Pacific Plate, hosts mineralization dominated by a stratabound deposit architecture and locally overprinted by a tungsten skarn deposit, reflecting the interaction of synvolcanic hydrothermal processes, stratigraphic controls, and later metasomatic fluid circulation.

The Ertsberg deposit, Cortez deposit, and Dapingzhang deposit represent geologically distinct mineral systems, with the Ertsberg deposit characterized by a resource of contained copper of 0.35 Mt, the Cortez deposit hosting high-grade gold mineralization at 3.8 g/t Au, and the Dapingzhang deposit containing lithium enrichment of 240 ppm Li, collectively illustrating the spatial and genetic variability of ore-forming processes.

In mineralized terranes, kaolinization commonly overprints dolostone and shale adjacent to the ore system, whereas outward zoning may progress through a chlorite zone into a propylitic zone and locally an albitized zone, reflecting progressive fluid–rock interaction, depletion of ferromagnesian phases, and increasing metasomatic sodic alteration.

The geological specimen was prepared as a thin section and analyzed alongside a sphalerite sample by X-ray diffractometer to constrain mineral assemblages, while microthermometric measurement of fluid inclusions and Ti-in-zircon thermometry were integrated to quantify ore-forming temperatures and thereby characterize the thermal evolution of the mineral deposit.

Flat slab subduction along the southeastern margin of the Junggar Basin is interpreted to have modified the mantle wedge, promoting anomalous magmatic-hydrothermal fluxes that may have contributed to the formation and localization of the Malmberget deposit and the Neves-Corvo deposit through enhanced crustal metallogeny and focused fluid pathways.

In the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau and Cathaysia Block, remote sensing identified a TM iron-oxide anomaly and associated iron-oxide anomaly and OH-bearing mineral anomaly that spatially coincide with a carbonate zone and sericite zone, suggesting hydrothermal alteration linked to mineralizing fluids in a back-arc basin setting and thereby delineating prospective mineral deposit targets.

In a polished section from the Tenke Fungurume deposit, fluorapatite occurs as a primary phosphate phase associated with zincite and illite in ore-grade assemblages, whereas at the Jwaneng deposit these minerals are absent, reflecting contrasting paragenetic histories and host-rock geochemical controls on mineral deposition and alteration.

In the intracratonic basin of Europe, the Monterey Formation’s productive member, a carbonate formation, is spatially juxtaposed with sericite-quartz schist, suggesting that lithological contrasts and stratigraphic compartmentalization within these rock units may have controlled fluid migration pathways and thus localized mineral deposition.

The Jinchuan deposit contains a supergene orebody whose geometry and enrichment pattern are strongly controlled by a thrust fault, which acted as a structural conduit and localized deformation zone, thereby influencing fluid migration, secondary sulfide accumulation, and the spatial distribution of high-grade mineralization within the ore system.

The Veladero deposit is characterized by geological resources including proven and probable reserves of 10.5 Mt, with a probable reserve of 7.9 Mt at 1.05% Cu and an ore reserve of 6.1 Mt, while its mineralization exhibits grades of 240 ppm Li, 0.35% WO3, and 1.4% Cu equivalent, collectively indicating a polymetallic system of significant economic potential.

text: Strontium isotopes and zinc isotopes together constrain the evolution of the high-salinity ore fluid, indicating that mineral precipitation occurred during interaction with crustal sources under contrasting thermal regimes, with one episode at 180–240 °C and a later, higher-temperature stage at 281 to 371 °C, thereby recording a complex multi-stage hydrothermal mineralizing system.

In Laurasia, quartz arenite and slate host structurally controlled mineralization, whereas in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Southeast Asia the Mount Isa deposit and Obuasi deposit exemplify contrasting ore-forming systems in which lithological competence contrasts and regional tectonics govern fluid flow, sulfide precipitation, and the localization of economically significant ore bodies.

Mineral deposit formation is commonly attributed to a 250-320 °C mineralizing fluid, specifically a N2-bearing hydrothermal fluid with very low salinity of 1.5 wt.% NaCl equiv., whose evolution can be constrained by 187Re/188Os geochronology together with hafnium isotopes and molybdenum isotopes, thereby linking fluid source, metal transport, and ore precipitation within the hydrothermal fluid system.

spans.text: very low salinity of 1.5 wt.% NaCl equiv.

text: Mineral-fluid inclusion data indicate that the ore-forming system evolved from an aqueous fluid to an N2-bearing hydrothermal fluid and ultimately a magmatic-hydrothermal fluid, with homogenization temperature of 280 °C, trapping temperature of 300 °C, and a broader thermal interval of 250–320 °C corresponding to mineral entrapment under a trapping pressure of 1.2–2.0 kbar, approximately 1.5 kbar, and a pressure range of 0.8–1.6 kbar.

The massive sulfide orebody is localized along a contact boundary and is spatially associated with a sheeted vein system, with the principal structural trend defined by a NNE strike that is equivalent to a strike of N20E, while subordinate mineralized zones also extend along a NW-trending fabric, indicating structurally controlled ore emplacement.

The Pulang deposit in Hebei is characterized by a Cu-Pb-Zn geochemical anomaly that spatially delineates mineralized hydrothermal alteration zones, whereas the Tampakan deposit on the African Plate is associated with a negative geochemical anomaly, reflecting elemental depletion in surrounding host rocks and emphasizing contrasting ore-forming geochemical signatures between the two mineral systems.

The Malmberget deposit, located in the central mining area of the Cripple Creek mining district and contrasted with mineralized systems of the Tibetan Plateau, exemplifies a structurally controlled ore concentration in which lithological contrasts, tectonic deformation, and hydrothermal alteration collectively governed the localization and preservation of economically significant mineralization.

Within the metallogenic belt, the mining camp is characterized by a geochemical anomaly zone in which a positive geochemical anomaly of rubidium delineates a prospective mineralization corridor, suggesting hydrothermal enrichment processes and providing a robust geochemical vector for targeting concealed mineral deposits.

In the Tarkwa deposit and Norilsk deposit, structural controls such as a transform fault, decollement, and chevron fold localize mineralization by focusing fluid flow into a stockwork orebody and ore pipe, where evaporitic and metasomatic processes precipitate halite, magnesite, and cerussite within structurally prepared dilation zones.

The Olympic Dam deposit and the Antamina deposit exemplify contrasting ore-forming systems, with the former locally characterized by tungsten grades of 0.35% WO3 and trace-element concentrations ranging from 120-180 ppm, while the latter contains copper mineralization averaging 1.25% Cu, with an associated geochemical anomaly approximately 150 ppm and an altered zone extending from 12 to 28 m.

The vein sample, mineralized rock sample, and calcite sample collectively record hydrothermal alteration in syenogranite, marble, and hornfels, respectively, indicating that mineralizing fluids exploited structurally prepared lithologies and produced localized replacement, veining, and metasomatic modification consistent with the development of a polymetallic mineral deposit.

The Triassic granitoid plutons and pillow basalt were overprinted by hydrothermal alteration, whereby a CaCl2-rich basinal brine and a NaCl-CaCl2 brine, subsequently interacting with a CO2-bearing aqueous fluid, facilitated metal mobilization and carbonate precipitation to form smithsonite mineralization within structurally controlled zones.

In the ignimbrite host rock, propylitic alteration, albitization, and sericitization are interpreted to reflect successive interaction with CO2-bearing aqueous fluid, inclusion fluid, and high-salinity ore fluid, respectively, which together facilitated the paragenesis of tantalite, talc, and tennantite within a structurally focused hydrothermal system.

In the Philippines, a stratigraphic succession comprising an evaporite formation overlain by a limestone bed is spatially associated with rock units of amphibolite, phosphorite, and rhyolite, suggesting structurally controlled mineral deposition and diagenetic remobilization within a tectonically complex sedimentary-volcanic basin system.

In Central America, mineralization analogous to the Abitibi gold belt and Carlin gold belt is commonly linked to Triassic and Carboniferous tectonomagmatic events, whereas the Cannington deposit, Tara deposit, and Tenke Fungurume deposit exemplify structurally focused ore systems whose economic enrichment was further modified during Neogene reactivation and basin evolution.

In the Th-Pb system, isotopic constraints indicate that the mineralizing fluid, an H2O-CO2-NaCl fluid, was trapped at a temperature of 300 °C, suggesting that ore precipitation occurred under moderately high-temperature hydrothermal conditions and that the fluid’s mixed volatile-saline composition played a key role in metal transport and deposition.

The ore-bearing drill core was subjected to U-Pb dating and stable isotope analysis, and the resulting geochronological and isotopic constraints were integrated with FTIR spectrometer measurements to characterize mineral paragenesis, identify alteration minerals, and refine the temporal evolution of the deposit-forming hydrothermal system.

Mineralization is interpreted to have formed from a H2O-CO2-NaCl fluid and later metamorphic fluid, as indicated by a trapping temperature of 345 C and a temperature ranges from 98.2 to 135 °C, with salinity of 4.8 wt.% NaCl equiv.; isotopic constraints from 238U/206Pb geochronology and chromium isotopes further delineate the timing and redox evolution of the mineral deposits.

The Jiangbian deposit, Las Bambas deposit, and Talnakh deposit exemplify contrasting ore systems in which the Jiangbian deposit reports an average grade of 1.6% Cu, Las Bambas is characterized by an inferred resource of 12 Mt and a cutoff grade of 0.5% Cu, and Talnakh contains an indicated resource of 8.6 Mt with an ore grade of 4.2 g/t Au, thereby illustrating spatially discrete metallogenic controls and grade variability.

The Re-Os system has been applied to mineralogical and fluid-inclusion studies of smithsonite-bearing assemblages from the Olympic Dam deposit, where isotopic signatures help constrain the timing of hydrothermal mineralization and subsequent supergene overprinting within this giant iron-oxide Cu-U-Au-REE ore system.

Mineral deposition was governed by crustal extension, oxidative leaching, and regional metamorphism, whereby a CH4-bearing fluid interacted with host rocks at an ore fluid temperature of 290 °C, evolved toward a homogenization temperature of 165 C, and was subsequently overprinted by metamorphic recrystallization at a peak temperature of 420 °C, collectively controlling metal mobilization and precipitation.

The Aitik deposit and the Escondida deposit are both associated with a positive geophysical anomaly and a chargeability anomaly, reflecting sulfide-rich mineralization that is commonly localized along a thrust fault and, in some districts, within or adjacent to a diatreme, where these structures enhance hydrothermal fluid focusing and ore deposition.

In the Devonian wall rocks adjacent to the biotite granite, mineralization is characterized by microcline associated with tourmalinization, smectite developed through argillization, and aragonite precipitated during late-stage carbonatization, collectively indicating a multistage hydrothermal overprint and progressive fluid–rock interaction within the mineral deposit system.

Rodingite adjacent to hornblende gabbro and enclosing wall rock records intense wall-rock alteration, during which a NaCl-CaCl2 brine, a CO2-bearing aqueous fluid, and an H2O-CO2 fluid circulated through fractures and reaction zones, promoting metasomatic replacement, phlogopite crystallization, and localized precipitation of native silver within the mineralized system.

In the mineral deposit study, a quartz-vein sample and associated chip sample were analyzed through a soil geochemical survey, with the resulting anomaly patterns refined by SEM imaging and examined petrographically using a cathodoluminescence microscope, while volatile and organic signatures were quantified with a gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer to constrain ore-forming processes and alteration zonation.

The clastic group and limestone formation, each associated with calc-silicate rock as a key host or contact-metasedimentary lithology, are interpreted as favorable stratigraphic and lithological controls on mineral deposition across South Asia, the South China Block, and South Africa, where regional metamorphism and fluid-rock interaction have concentrated ore-forming elements along permeable horizons and reactive carbonate-silicate interfaces.

At the Grasberg deposit, a gravity survey delineated density contrasts consistent with concealed ore geometry, while comparative analysis of wall-rock sample, mineralized rock sample, and quartz-vein sample showed that azurite and franklinite are preferentially concentrated in the mineralized domains and vein selvages, thereby constraining the spatial relationship between hydrothermal alteration, veining, and sulfide-oxide mineralization.

The Pebble deposit, Shizhuyuan deposit, and Gejiu deposit are interpreted as volcanogenic massive sulfide deposit systems, with the Pebble deposit commonly compared to a Kuroko-type deposit and the Shizhuyuan and Gejiu deposits spatially or genetically associated with alkali feldspar granite intrusions that may have contributed heat, fluids, or metal fertility to mineralization.

In British Columbia, remote sensing identified a calcite anomaly and epidote anomaly spatially associated with a mineralized alteration zone that grades into a ferric iron alteration zone and a pyritic zone, while analogous alteration assemblages in the Texas rift basin suggest that such geochemical and mineralogical signatures may mark structurally controlled mineral deposit emplacement.

The mineralized system comprises a stratabound orebody that transitions laterally into a disseminated orebody and vertically into a stratiform orebody, centered at UTM Easting 345678, located at 31.25 N, 103.47 E, where drilling to a depth of 1200 m has delineated a down-dip extent of 400 m, indicating substantial subsurface continuity and structural control.

Mineralogical and geochemical data indicate that quartz, alunite, and smectite assemblages are associated with hydrothermal alteration in a mineral deposit characterized by 5.4 g/t Au and 12.6% TFe, with fluid inclusion and trace-element constraints suggesting formation temperatures of 220–260 °C and ore-forming fluids containing 120–180 ppm of dissolved constituents.

At the McArthur River deposit, the silicic zone of the hydrothermal alteration halo is characterized by pervasive silica enrichment and mineralogical overprinting, where skutterudite is associated with ore-stage assemblages, goethite occurs as a supergene oxidation product, and biotite marks proximal wall-rock alteration reflecting elevated-temperature hydrothermal conditions.

The main gold-mineralizing stage of the metallogenic time was likely controlled by magmatic intrusion and subsequent regional metamorphism, which collectively promoted hydrothermal alteration and recrystallization, with hornblende reflecting mafic protoliths or contact-metamorphic assemblages, gypsum indicating late-stage sulfate deposition from oxidizing fluids, and albite recording sodic metasomatism within the evolving mineral system.

The Neves-Corvo deposit, Red Dog deposit, and Norilsk deposit exemplify contrasting metallogenic systems, with the former characterized by a 7.5% Zn grade, the latter by 1.1% Pb, and the Norilsk deposit by 0.35% WO3, while the associated mineral inventory includes a measured and indicated resource of 36 Mt, an inferred resource of 24.6 Mt at 0.72% Cu, and contained gold of 42 t, collectively indicating substantial polymetallic endowment and continuity at depth.

text: Sulfur isotopes preserved in the ore deposit provide critical constraints on the source and evolution of the ore-forming fluid, indicating that metal transport and sulfide precipitation occurred under a peak temperature of 510 C during hydrothermal mineralization.

In the upper member, the orebody of the Platreef forms a structurally controlled saddle reef that is localized within a recumbent fold, where intense deformation and associated stratigraphic repetition enhanced fluid focusing and created favorable sites for mineral precipitation.

During the Silurian, hydrothermal and tectonotectonic processes in New South Wales are inferred to have contributed to the metallogenesis of porphyry-style mineralization, providing a regional temporal framework for comparing the Linong-Luong Cu deposit, the Alumbrera deposit, and the Dapingzhang deposit within broader Cu-ore-forming systems.

Shearing along lithospheric-scale structures facilitated fluid flow and focused hydrothermal remobilization, whereby syenite protoliths were variably converted to greisen and, locally, granulite, while superimposed hematitization and advanced argillic alteration overprinted the assemblage, collectively indicating a structurally controlled mineralizing system.

The Tianshan metallogenic belt in Xinjiang, East Asia, is characterized by spatially and temporally diverse mineralization, including porphyry copper deposits, sedimentary exhalative deposits, and epithermal gold-silver deposits, whereas the Bingham Canyon mining district, although tectonically distinct, exemplifies a giant porphyry copper deposit system formed through comparable magmatic-hydrothermal processes.

At the Batu Hijau deposit, a low-resistivity anomaly and associated IP anomaly define a broader resistivity anomaly that spatially coincides with a transtensional shear zone, indicating that structurally focused hydrothermal alteration and sulfide mineralization were concentrated along this high-permeability corridor.

In the garnet-sillimanite gneiss, mineralization is spatially associated with a greisen zone developed through greisenization, whereas adjacent decarbonatization and epidotization are concentrated within the carbonate alteration zone, together recording a progressive hydrothermal overprint that redistributed silica, alkalis, and volatile components and enhanced ore-forming permeability.

Lithospheric delamination may initiate asthenospheric upwelling and water-fluxed melting, whereas subsequent fractional crystallization of the evolving magmas can drive hydrothermal differentiation during stage II mineralization and the quartz-carbonate stage, thereby promoting the deposition of enargite and calaverite within structurally controlled ore systems.

The Los Bronces deposit, situated along the northern margin of Oceania in Colorado, is hosted by anorthosite intruding into or juxtaposed against country rock, where the contrasting lithologies and associated structural controls are inferred to have influenced ore localization, fluid focusing, and subsequent mineralization within the broader magmatic-hydrothermal system.

The Pueblo Viejo deposit and Porgera deposit are characterized by high-grade ore zones, and the resource inventory includes contained gold of 0.70 t, whereas economic delineation may be constrained by a cutoff grade of 0.5% Cu, reflecting the spatial distribution of mineralization and the metallurgical and geostatistical parameters governing ore classification.

In Qinghai and northeastern China, remote-sensing anomalies characterized by alunite and carbonate signatures delineate an alteration halo encompassing a propylitic zone and an advanced argillic zone, while their spatial association with an island arc tectonic setting indicates hydrothermal mineralization processes typical of epithermal to porphyry-related mineral deposits.

Sulfur isotopes and δ97Mo values from the mineralized assemblage indicate deposition from a CH4-bearing fluid under reduced redox conditions, with Th values of 220–260 C constraining ore formation to moderate-temperature hydrothermal circulation in which fluid-rock interaction likely controlled metal transport and precipitation.

Stage III mineralization in the Northparkes deposit and Cadia deposit records a protracted metallogenic evolution, with an early mineralization age of 318 Ma followed by overprinting events at 126 ± 2 Ma and a younger mineralization age of 95–88 Ma, collectively indicating multiple discrete hydrothermal pulses and temporal reactivation of the magmatic-hydrothermal system.

In the Jinshajiang suture zone, the Simandou deposit is spatially associated with a radiometric anomaly and a high-resistivity anomaly, while its ore bodies are structurally controlled by an overturned fold and a synform, indicating that post-collisional deformation and lithological contrasts jointly enhanced mineral concentration.

During Laramide tectonism, hydrothermal circulation drove metallogenesis across the district, first concentrating ore metals in the quartz-sulfide stage and later overprinting them during the W-Sn mineralization stage, thereby generating tennantite and native copper assemblages and promoting supergene oxidation to cerussite in the evolving mineral deposits.

The South orebody, comprising a massive sulfide lens forming the principal ore column, is situated at UTM Zone 47N Northing 3298760, 29.75 N, and extends to 920 m depth, indicating a structurally controlled mineralized system with substantial vertical continuity and significant implications for subsurface resource estimation.

The Cortez deposit is characterized by a grade of 850 ppb Au and reported proven and probable reserves of 10.5 Mt, whereas the Chuquicamata deposit includes an ore reserve of 6.1 Mt and an ore reserve of 12.8 Mt at 28 g/t Ag, while a separate Pb-rich mineralized domain exhibits a grade of 1.1% Pb, collectively reflecting distinct metallogenic controls and resource classifications.
