ore reserve of 12.8 Mt at 28 g/t Ag

text: In a breccia pipe deposit or epithermal deposit, a disseminated zone may overprint a gossan orebody and adjacent lode structures, where hydrothermal alteration and supergene oxidation localize accessory mineralization of kyanite in the gangue and economically significant concentrations of pyrochlore and sphalerite within the orebody.

Mineralization is spatially associated with an angular unconformity that truncates the clastic group, while a fault zone containing a drag fold locally focuses fluid flow into an ash bed, where stringer zone development grades upward into layered and veined Cu orebodies and culminates in the Upper orebody, reflecting structurally controlled hydrothermal remobilization.

Integrated IP survey, in situ U-Pb geochronology, and cathodoluminescence imaging of the mineral deposit, together with δ11B, uranium isotopes, and δ44Ca analyses, constrain the paragenesis and fluid evolution, indicating that the molybdenite Re-Os age of 160.3 ± 1.7 Ma records an earlier magmatic-hydrothermal event, whereas the ore-forming age of 73–56 Ma reflects subsequent mineralization and metallogenic reactivation.

The Yulong deposit, situated within an active margin setting in North America and influenced by the Antarctic Plate, is interpreted to have formed through slab breakoff-driven magmatism followed by intense shearing, which collectively enhanced fluid focusing, structural permeability, and ore-fluid transport necessary for mineral concentration.

Magmatic-hydrothermal fluid exsolution commonly promotes wall-rock alteration, silicification, and greisenization, during which interaction with reactive host rocks can precipitate magnesite and induce replacement or metasomatic overprinting of pyroxene, thereby generating distinctive mineral assemblages characteristic of hydrothermal mineral deposits.

Geological mapping of a mineralized rock sample and a vein quartz sample, coupled with electron backscatter diffraction analysis on an SEM, zircon U-Pb geochronology, and geochemical screening using a portable XRF analyzer and ICP-OES spectrometer, constrains the timing, deformation history, and metal distribution of the mineral deposit with high spatial and compositional precision.

Integrated zircon saturation thermometry, EPMA analysis, and geochronological analysis of the mineral deposits indicates magmatic temperatures consistent with zircon crystallization and constrains emplacement through the U-Pb system, yielding a zircon U-Pb age of 246 ± 4 Ma, a 235 ± 3 Ma episode, and a broader 186–172 Ma interval that collectively records multistage hydrothermal-mineralization evolution.

The Voisey’s Bay deposit and Cuajone deposit are characteristically associated with magmatic and porphyry-style mineralization, whereas the Venetia deposit is hosted within a breccia pipe, and the Comstock Lode orebody is genetically linked to a hydrothermal breccia pipe; additionally, structural control by a parasitic fold may localize fluid pathways and enhance ore deposition within the broader breccia pipe system.

The magnetic anomaly identified by geophysical surveying is interpreted to reflect a lenticular orebody, whose orebody depth ranges from 200 to 500 m and includes a principal depth of 450 m, suggesting a structurally controlled mineralized lens emplaced at moderate crustal levels.

spans.text: orebody depth ranges from 200 to 500 m

text: The Broken Hill deposit is characterized by a tabular orebody, the Cigar Lake deposit by an ore horizon, and the Ranger deposit by mineralization associated with a resurgent caldera, while the Main Reef Leader exemplifies a structurally controlled orebody localized along a shear band and further modified by a drag fold, reflecting the strong tectonic influence on ore deposition.

In the ore sample, epidotization and decarbonatization are interpreted as metasomatic alteration assemblages affecting the peridotite and adjacent pyroxene hornfels, indicating fluid-assisted mineral redistribution and reaction-driven replacement that enhanced the concentration of ore-forming components within the deposit.

The H2O-NaCl fluid precipitated pyrite and anhydrite within the ore assemblage, while concomitant wall-rock alteration and silicification overprinted the host strata and locally stabilized pentlandite-bearing zones, indicating focused hydrothermal flow, saline fluid–rock interaction, and progressive physicochemical evolution of the mineralizing system.

Metamorphism in the Superior Craton has exerted a profound control on ore-forming systems, influencing the thermal and structural evolution that helped localize the Cigar Lake deposit and Ranger deposit, while also contributing to the metallogenic setting of the Pulang deposit through regional deformation, fluid migration, and mineralization processes.

During the Latest Cretaceous–Early Palaeocene interval, obsidian-hosted hydrothermal mineralization developed along structural discontinuities cutting the carbonate sequence and the Longmaxi Formation, where late-stage fluid circulation promoted silica overprinting, vein infill, and localized metal enrichment within adjacent fracture networks.

The Deep orebody, a hydrothermal breccia-pipe orebody at coordinates of 28°15′N, 105°30′E (103.47°E; UTM Zone 47N Northing 3298760), extends to 650 m below surface and exhibits a down-plunge extent of 700 m, indicating a vertically extensive mineralized system controlled by brecciation and focused hydrothermal fluid flow.

Supergene enrichment of the mineral deposit was enhanced by fault reactivation that focused fluid flow along the contact between calcareous siltstone and syenogranite, where tourmaline-quartz alteration overprinted the host rocks and localized ore-bearing assemblages adjacent to the underlying pillow basalt.

Phlogopite mineralization within the rhyolite rock unit is commonly associated with hydrothermal alteration characterized by argillization and chloritization, processes that reflect progressive feldspar and mafic mineral replacement under fluid-rock interaction and thereby provide important geochemical indicators of epithermal to mesothermal mineral deposit evolution.

The Shizhuyuan deposit is interpreted as a mineralized system whose geological context can be contrasted with placer deposit concentrations in quartz arenite, carbonatite-hosted deposit mineralization, and bauxite deposit accumulation in quartzite and arkose, thereby illustrating the strong dependence of ore formation on lithological composition, provenance, and depositional or magmatic processes.

The Platreef ore body is characterized by a sheeted vein system emplaced within a shear zone exhibiting a northwest strike, where structurally controlled dilation and deformation focused hydrothermal fluid flow to concentrate mineralization along discrete, subparallel veins.

The Main orebody is localized at the lithological contact within a diatreme, where a hydrothermal breccia-pipe orebody and subordinate vein-type orebody exploit permeability contrasts in an ore-hosting sequence, and individual mineralized beds occur within a coarsening-upward sequence, indicating structural focusing of mineralizing fluids along stratigraphically and lithologically favorable horizons.

In the graywacke and quartzite host rocks, hydrothermal alteration generated montmorillonite and anorthite assemblages, while stibnite precipitated from a mineralizing fluid of basinal brine affinity, as indicated by inclusion fluid signatures that record episodic fluid-rock interaction, metal transport, and subsequent sulfide deposition within structurally controlled mineral deposits.

The UG2 Reef, a stratiform orebody, and the Lower orebody, together with the hypogene orebody, exemplify distinct mineralized horizons that may be spatially and genetically associated with a greisen tin-tungsten deposit or greisen deposit, whereas a sediment-hosted stratiform copper deposit can locally contain halite as an evaporitic gangue mineral within basin-controlled mineralization systems.

Fluid-inclusion and geothermometric constraints indicate that the mineral deposits evolved under a thermal regime characterized by a peak temperature of 510 °C, a trapping temperature of 345 °C, and a homogenization temperature of 280 °C, while pressure estimates range from an estimated trapping pressure of 2.4 kbar to 1.5 kbar and locally below 1 kbar, over an inferred mineralizing interval of Ma.

In the Jinding deposit, the alteration halo is classically zoned from an outer propylitic zone inward to an albitic zone and locally a hematite-rich zone, where diopside-bearing assemblages record higher-temperature skarn overprinting, whereas orpiment in the Malmberget deposit and the Resolution deposit reflects late-stage hydrothermal sulfide deposition under more reduced conditions.

The Altai metallogenic belt, a polymetallic belt, and the East Kunlun metallogenic belt, together with the Kansanshi deposit in Inner Mongolia, the Rössing deposit on the Fennoscandian Shield, and the Boddington deposit, exemplify metallogenically diverse ore systems whose emplacement, structural control, and hydrothermal overprinting reflect large-scale crustal evolution and regional tectono-magmatic processes.

The mineralization comprises a saddle reef OreBody and an associated hanging-wall orebody, with the former centered near UTM Northing 3789123 and the latter constrained within UTM Zone 47N Northing 3298760, such that the orebody depth ranges from 200 to 500 m, includes a burial depth of 300 m, and locally extends to 920 m depth, indicating substantial vertical persistence.

text: The gossan orebody is exposed over 300 m and exhibits a steeply dipping geometry, with a mineralized width of 14 m and an estimated true width of 7.5 m locally narrowing to 5 m, while structural measurements indicate variable attitude from a shallow dip of 25° to a near-vertical dip along strike.

The disseminated orebody and associated orebody occur within the Mount Isa Group terrestrial sequence, where the ore seam is localized along a conjugate fault system that facilitated fluid focusing, structural dilation, and subsequent stratabound mineral deposition under favorable syntectonic conditions.

At the Husab deposit, the syn-ore stage of mineralization at an ore-forming age of 158 ± 2 Ma records the primary metallogenic event, whereas the Pueblo Viejo deposit and Red Dog deposit are better interpreted as products of a later post-mineralization stage, with the latter constrained by a significantly older metallogenic signal of 318 Ma.

GeophysicalAnomaly data, including a gravitational anomaly, electrical anomaly, and linear EM anomaly, when integrated with Structural controls such as the Jinshajiang suture zone, detachment fault, and caldera architecture, provide a robust framework for targeting the Kiruna deposit, Cigar Lake deposit, and Los Bronces deposit, where mineralization is spatially associated with discrete tectonic and magmatic loci.

The Sishen deposit, hosted predominantly in banded iron formation rather than the specified rock units, contrasts with the Voisey’s Bay deposit, a classic magmatic nickel-copper-PGE deposit genetically linked to mafic-ultramafic intrusion, and the Cadia deposit, an intrusion-related gold deposit associated with calc-alkaline magmatism; by comparison, SEDEX mineralization is typically stratabound in siltstone, whereas eclogite more commonly records high-pressure metamorphic terranes than direct ore hosting.

In the Tintina Gold Belt, a negative geochemical anomaly in stream-sediment anomaly data, including a Bi-W anomaly and elevated Ni and Sb signatures, can delineate mineralized source rocks within the mining district and help vector exploration toward concealed ore systems in the Tintic mining district.

In the metasedimentary sequence, a blind thrust fault and associated brittle shear zone localized deformation and fluid flow, resulting in the development of a hanging-wall orebody within the productive member above the lower member, while the Merensky Reef was displaced and structurally juxtaposed, thereby enhancing ore concentration along the principal mineralized horizon.

The Gejiu deposit, characterized by a grade of 850 ppb Au and associated with a proven reserve of 2.4 Mt at 3.6 g/t Au, contrasts with the Escondida deposit, which hosts a probable reserve of 7.9 Mt at 1.05% Cu, while the Talnakh deposit, notable for 1.1% Pb mineralization, contains a proven and probable reserve of 9.7 Mt, collectively reflecting distinct metallogenic systems and ore-forming processes.

text: Mineral deposition is commonly interpreted to result from tectonism and slab breakoff, which promote volatile-mediated lower-crustal melting and the generation of a magmatic-hydrothermal fluid, with fluid inclusion constraints indicating a homogenization temperature of 165 C, a Th of 135 °C, and a trapping temperature of 300 °C, thereby recording progressive cooling and ore-forming precipitation.

The mineralized collapse breccia pipe is characterized by a pronounced NNE strike and a steep dip of 80 degrees, indicating a structurally controlled conduit that likely focused hydrothermal fluid flow and facilitated ore deposition within the brecciated host rock.

The mineral deposit records interaction between a CO2-rich fluid and inclusion fluid during ore formation at an ore fluid temperature of 290 °C within a broader 250–320 °C interval, as indicated by a 143Nd/144Nd isotopic signature and fluid-inclusion salinities ranging from very low salinity of 1.5 wt.% NaCl equiv. to brine salinity of 12.4 wt.% and 22.0 wt.% NaCl equiv., reflecting episodic mixing and fluid evolution.

text: Franklinite and tantalite commonly occur as accessory to ore-grade mineralization in metamorphic gneiss, whereas chrysotile is typically developed within serpentinite during hydration and low-temperature alteration; locally, sericitization overprints feldspathic domains and may further redistribute compatible elements, thereby recording a complex paragenetic sequence linking regional metamorphism, ultramafic alteration, and hydrothermal modification.

Mineral deposits in subduction-related orogenic systems may form where magmatism and crustal thickening drive the circulation of a CO2-bearing aqueous fluid, with tourmaline and biotite recording fluid–rock interaction under an ore fluid temperature of 290 C and broader hydrothermal conditions spanning 281 to 371 °C, although later alteration may reflect temperature ranges from 98.2 to 135 °C.

In the Witwatersrand gold field and adjoining gold belt, a pronounced Au anomaly, together with Cu-Mo anomaly and multi-element anomaly signatures, is commonly interpreted as reflecting hydrothermal mineralization and ore-fluid dispersion, while elevated Cs, Ir, and lead concentrations may indicate element remobilization, magmatic input, or sulfide-associated metal enrichment within structurally controlled mineral deposits.

The ore body defines an ore horizon at 31.25°N, 103.47°E, where systematic drilling indicates a down-plunge extent of 700 m and a maximum depth of 450 m, suggesting a structurally controlled mineralized system with significant vertical continuity and lateral persistence.

During the Latest Cretaceous–Early Palaeocene, mineralizing fluids interacted with the basement sequence, intruding volcanic formation dykes of lamprophyre and volcaniclastic volcanic breccia, and were subsequently focused along contacts with the Brockman Iron Formation and permeable oolitic limestone, thereby promoting structurally controlled precipitation and localized enrichment of ore minerals.

The Voisey’s Bay deposit, a magmatic sulfide deposit, is spatially associated with gabbroic intrusions and tectonically juxtaposed schist, whereas the coexisting epizonal vein deposit and IOCG deposit are plausibly linked to late-stage hydrothermal remobilization along greisen-altered structures, reflecting a complex magmatic-hydrothermal evolution.

The Comstock Lode ore vein, an OreBody situated near 31.25°N, 103.47°E at UTM Northing 3789123 and UTM Easting 345678, extends to 650 m below surface, where its geometry, continuity, and depth persistence indicate a structurally controlled hydrothermal mineral deposit.

The breccia orebody may represent a structurally focused mineralized conduit within a lode gold deposit, a Broken Hill-type deposit, or an iron oxide copper-gold deposit, where hydrothermal fluids precipitate copper-bearing secondary minerals such as chrysocolla in fracture networks and matrix-supported breccias, thereby recording late-stage supergene or hypogene alteration.

At the Neves-Corvo deposit, the sulfide assemblage locally contains pyroxene and augite in the altered host rocks, while phlogopite may record metasomatic potassic enrichment; trace-element analyses further indicate anomalous indium, Se, and Hf concentrations associated with hydrothermal mineralization and fluid–rock interaction within the ore system.

Fluid inclusion and isotopic evidence indicate that the mineralizing fluid was a basinal brine that evolved under high-temperature hydrothermal conditions, with a trapping temperature of 300 °C within a broader homogenization range of 281 to 371 °C and a secondary Th of 135 °C, while its salinity varied from moderate salinity of 6 wt.% NaCl equiv. to 8–12 wt.% NaCl equiv., consistent with episodic fluid mixing and focusing during ore deposition.

text: In the Los Bronces deposit, chalcopyrite constitutes a प्रमुख copper sulfide phase associated with Mo-bearing hydrothermal assemblages, while rhodonite occurs as a manganese silicate gangue mineral, together documenting polyphase alteration and ore-fluid evolution characteristic of porphyry-style mineralization with localized metal zoning.
